blocks.text: Dear Ms. Campos:

This letter is our agreement to carry out an operational review for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}, and if you sign it and return one copy to me we will begin on the date set out below. It records what we will do, who will do it, what it costs, and what each of us owes the other, and it is short because I have never seen a long one prevent a disagreement. If any part of it reads differently from what we discussed, say so before you sign rather than after, since the whole value of a letter like this is that you and I are holding the same one.
Scope and Approach

The review covers how the work of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} actually gets done, from the point an order arrives through purchasing, scheduling, production, and shipment, together with the reporting and the standing meetings your managers use to steer all of it. We will look for the places where work waits, where it is done twice, and where the numbers your people manage by have stopped describing what happens on the floor, and we will tell you which of those is costing you the most. We are not auditing your accounts, we are not evaluating individual employees, and we will not hand you a recommendation that would require a different company than the one you have.

Our method is ordinary and we have used it for years. We start in your facilities rather than in your data, because a morning spent watching a shift and asking a supervisor what gets in his way is worth more at the outset than a month of analysis built on somebody else's summary, and we then work from the reporting your systems already produce rather than asking your staff to build anything new for us. We will interview across the business, from your leadership down to the people who touch the product, and we will test what we are told against what we watch, because in our experience the two disagree at first and the gap between them is where the money sits. At the end you get a written report with a short set of recommendations, each carrying the change it asks for and the argument for it, and we will sit with your leadership team and take their questions on every one.
The Team

Robert Miller will lead the review and will be in your facilities for most of it. He works from the floor up, he is slow to accept a conclusion he cannot trace back to something he watched happen, and he is content to let a conversation go quiet until the useful thing gets said. Jennifer Fletcher will run the engagement week to week, hold the workplan, and write to you as the work moves; she is plain on paper, and when a finding is going to be uncomfortable she puts it in the first paragraph instead of at the back where you might miss it. Our analysts will do the supporting work under their supervision and will not be in front of you without one of them. My signature is on this letter and I read what leaves this firm, so if at any point the work is not what you were promised, call me and I will deal with it myself.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for the review is {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}}, fixed for the scope described above, so if the work takes us longer than we have planned that is our problem and not your invoice. We bill in equal monthly installments across the term, the first on commencement, and each invoice is payable within thirty days of receipt. Travel and other out-of-pocket costs are billed at what they cost us and itemized on the invoice, and we will ask you first before we incur anything of consequence. If you want work that falls outside the scope above, we will describe it and price it in writing and you will approve it before we start, because we do not add scope during the work and settle it on an invoice afterward.
Term and Termination

The engagement commences on {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}} and runs approximately three months, which is the basis on which we have staffed it and priced it. Either of us may end it on thirty days' written notice. If you end it, you owe the installments that have fallen due and the approved costs we have incurred as of that date, and nothing beyond that. If we end it, which we would do only if we could not do the work honestly or were being asked for a conclusion we had not reached, we will return any fee already paid for work not yet performed.
Confidentiality

What you show us stays with us. We will use your information only for this engagement, we will hold it inside the group doing the work, and everyone on our side carries the same obligation I am putting in writing here. We will not name you as a client, in a proposal or anywhere else, without your written permission. We ask the same of you for our working papers, our methods, and our report, which remain ours and are given to you for the use of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} and not for circulation past it.
Limitation of Liability

We will carry out this work with the care and skill our profession expects, and we stand behind it. We do not control which of our recommendations you adopt or how your organization takes to them, so we do not warrant a saving, a rate, or any other outcome, and you should be wary of a firm that will. Our total liability under this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees you have actually paid us for it, and neither of us is liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss. Nothing in this paragraph limits liability for fraud or willful misconduct.
Governing Law

This letter is the entire agreement between us on this engagement and it replaces anything said or drafted before it. It is governed by the law of the state in which this firm keeps its principal office, and any dispute we cannot settle between ourselves belongs to the courts sitting in that state, to whose jurisdiction we each consent. It may be amended only in writing signed by both of us.
Acceptance

If this letter matches your understanding, please sign below and return one copy to me. Robert Miller will call your office to fix the kickoff and the first round of interviews, and Jennifer Fletcher will send the data request ahead of that so nothing waits on paperwork once we are on site. We are glad to be doing this work, and we will earn the letterhead it is written on.
Very truly yours,

To: Robert Miller
Cc: Michelle Lopez
From: Jennifer Fletcher
Re: Kickoff, operational review for {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}

The letter is signed and the term started {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}. This memo sets the objectives and the workstreams so we are both working from one plan. Our sponsor is Stephanie Campos, Chief Financial Officer at {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}. She asked for three things and we should write to those three. First, a clear picture of how work actually moves through the business. Second, a short list of the places where it stalls, ranked by what the stall costs. Third, recommendations her operating managers can act on without a translation step. Anything that does not serve one of those is out of scope. If the real problem turns out to sit somewhere we were not hired to look, we raise it with her early and in plain terms.
Workstreams

items: Order intake and scheduling. How an order becomes a commitment and a schedule, who is allowed to change it once it is set, and what a change costs downstream. Robert leads. I will sit in on the scheduling interviews.

Floor observation. Two full days per area, at shift start wherever we can get access, covering receiving, put-away, picking, and shipping. Robert. No conclusion in this workstream goes in the report unless one of us watched the thing happen.
Procurement and supplier terms. Lead times, order minimums, and how much of the scheduling problem is bought rather than made. Mine.
Management reporting. What is measured, what is reviewed, which of the standing meetings actually changes a decision, and where two reports give two answers to the same question. Mine.

Data and exhibits. Everything we pull from their systems goes into one workbook, with the source system and the date of the pull on each tab. We both work from that workbook and from no other copy. An exhibit that cannot name its tab does not go in front of the client.
Interim reporting. A short written status to the sponsor at the midpoint, and a note to her in between only when something changes that she would not want to hear about at a meeting. Mine.

blocks.text: Next Steps

Michelle Lopez has the calendar and the travel, and she cannot book anything until she has the site dates, which means your first two weeks. She also holds the engagement file. Everything that goes to the client goes out through her, so there is one record of what was sent and when. Nothing leaves this office over my name that Michelle has not logged.

Access is the thing most likely to cost us time. We need the shift starts, not a tour at ten in the morning, and we need the supervisors for an hour each without their manager in the room. I will ask Stephanie Campos for both directly rather than working it out with each area on the day. If she pushes back on the second one, tell me before you start rearranging the interview list, because it changes what the interviews are worth.

On the client side, I am going to send one consolidated data request instead of a stream of small ones. That is easier for her staff to fill and easier for us to chase, and it gives us a single list to point at when something is late. Assume everything on it takes two weeks to arrive and plan the analysis accordingly.

One more thing on tone. We are three days into a three-month engagement and we do not have findings, so we do not talk like we do. If a supervisor asks what we have found, the honest answer is that we are still watching.
Next step: send me your draft of the data request by the end of this week, and I will merge it with mine and get the combined list to Stephanie Campos on Monday morning.
Minutes: Operational Review Working Session, {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}
Held at the client's offices. Recorded by Robert Miller.
Attendees

items: Robert Miller, Principal Consultant, Pinebrook Advisory Group
Jennifer Fletcher, Senior Consultant, Pinebrook Advisory Group
Stephanie Campos, Chief Financial Officer, {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}}

blocks.text: Discussion

Stephanie Campos opened by asking where the work stands, and the answer we gave her is that observation in receiving, put-away, and picking is finished, the supervisor interviews are finished, and the order data is in hand and part way through reconciliation, which is far enough along to describe a pattern and not far enough along to put a number in front of her operating managers. She did not ask for a finding today and we did not offer one.

The substance of the hour was the distance between the schedule the floor works to and the schedule the system holds. Every supervisor we have sat with keeps his own version of the day, on paper or in a spreadsheet, and each of them gave the same reason for it, which is that the system schedule is set before the day's constraints are known and nobody goes back to correct it once the day starts moving. Jennifer Fletcher walked the group through what that does downstream, which is that the dates the sales office promises customers are built on a schedule the floor has already abandoned by mid-morning.

Stephanie Campos said her operating managers would dispute that characterization. We told her they may well be right in places, and that we will bring the paper schedules and the system extract to the next session and lay them beside each other so the argument happens over one document instead of two impressions.

Two items came out of the discussion that move our plan slightly. The first is that downtime is recorded in three places under three different definitions, and we cannot reconcile the count without somebody from her staff who knows why the definitions differ. The second is the returns process, which sits outside our scope as written and which keeps coming up in interviews as the reason picking gets interrupted. We asked whether she wanted it looked at. She said to look at it, and to keep it inside the current scope and fee, and Jennifer Fletcher will put the revised boundary in writing so nobody is guessing later about what was agreed in a room.
Outstanding from our side is nothing. Outstanding from theirs is the maintenance log extract and the promise-date history, both requested earlier this month and neither yet with us.
Action Items

rows: Release the maintenance log extract and the promise-date history
Name a member of finance staff to work the downtime definitions with us
Reconcile the three downtime definitions and document which one we will use
Confirm the returns question and the revised scope boundary in writing
Assemble the paper schedules and the system extract side by side

blocks.text: The next working session is set for the week after next at the client's offices, and the one thing that has to be true before it is that the maintenance extract is in our hands, because the downtime reconciliation is now the piece the rest of the operations analysis waits on.
Operational Review: Status Report
Prepared for the leadership of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.client}} by Jennifer Fletcher, Pinebrook Advisory Group.
Summary

We are at the midpoint of the review and the work is on schedule. It is also within the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2019-001.fee}}, and we do not expect to come back to you for more. Fieldwork in receiving, put-away, picking, and shipping is complete. Supervisor and planner interviews are complete. Analysis is under way, and the early findings are firm enough to describe but not yet firm enough to price, which is the right place to be at this point. One item has been added to scope at your request and it is absorbed in the current fee. Two data items are outstanding, and they are now the only real constraint on the schedule.
Completed This Period

items: Floor observation across receiving, put-away, picking, and shipping, at both shift starts. Robert Miller led this work and has been on site for most of it.
Interviews with supervisors, planners, and the sales office, covering every step at which an order is committed, scheduled, or changed.
Extraction and reconciliation of a full year of order and shipment data, held in a single workbook with the source and pull date recorded on each tab.
A working session at your offices to review progress, at which the returns process was added to our scope without a change in fee.
A first side-by-side comparison of the schedule the system holds and the schedule the floor actually works to.

blocks.text: Early Findings

These are directional. We will not stand behind a number until the outstanding data is in and reconciled, and we would ask you not to circulate them as conclusions yet. We set them out now because you should not meet them for the first time in a final report.

items: The system schedule is set before the day's constraints are known and is not corrected once the day begins. Every supervisor keeps a private version of it. The promise dates your sales office gives customers are built on the version nobody on the floor is using by mid-morning.
Downtime is recorded in three places under three definitions, so the business has three different answers to the same question and no way to settle which is right.
Interruptions to picking trace back to the returns process more often than to anything in the picking area itself, which is why we asked to bring returns inside the scope.
The standing operations meeting reviews a report that two of the people in the room do not use and cannot reproduce. That is a symptom of the definition problem above, not a separate one.

blocks.text: Method Note

One note on how we are working, because it affects how you should read what follows. We are not taking a figure from your reporting unless we can trace it to the transaction behind it. Where we cannot, we say so and we keep the figure out. That is slower than the alternative and it is the reason the operations analysis is waiting on an extract rather than proceeding on an estimate.
Work in the Next Period

items: Close the downtime reconciliation as soon as the maintenance extract arrives, and agree one definition with your staff before we publish any figure that uses it.
Quantify what schedule instability costs in rework, overtime, and expedited freight. This is the number your managers will argue with, so we will show the method beside the result.
Test the returns process against the picking interruptions it appears to cause.
Draft the recommendation set and walk it privately past your operating managers before it reaches paper. Robert Miller will run those conversations.
Deliver the written report and take your leadership team through it at the close of the engagement.

blocks.text: Risks

items: Data. The maintenance log extract and the promise-date history are still outstanding. The operations analysis waits on the first of them. This is the only item on this page that can move the delivery date.
Definitions. Until one downtime definition is agreed, any figure we publish will be argued on its definition instead of its substance, and the argument will not be a useful one.
Scope. The returns work is absorbed in the current fee, but it is absorbed by spending time that was held for contingency. A second addition of the same size would not be free.

Adoption. At least two of the recommendations we can already see will ask your operating managers to give up scheduling discretion they hold today. That is your decision rather than ours, and we will put it to you plainly when the time comes.

blocks.text: Request

The single thing that keeps this review on schedule is the maintenance log extract, which we asked for at the last working session and which the downtime reconciliation cannot start without. Please have it released to us by the end of next week. Robert Miller and I are on site through the coming period and can collect it in person if that is easier for your staff than sending it.
